coredata.dc:title: Atomically-tailored graphene oxide displaying enhanced fluorescence for the improved optical sensing of MMP-2
coredata.dc:creator: Yang, Jin-Kyoung
Hwang, In-Jun
Jeon, Su-Ji
Ju, Jong-Min
Kim, Hye-In
Yim, DaBin
Lee, Yoon-Sik
Kim, Jong-Ho
coredata.dc:description: Modulation of the optical properties of two-dimensional nanomaterials is a crucial step to improve their sensing performance for the optical detection of target molecules of interest. Here, we report a mild, simple and effective approach for heteroatoms-doping of graphene oxide (GO) without size alteration, resulting in enhanced fluorescence properties. Sulfur and nitrogen atoms were chemically doped onto the basal plane of GO (NSGO) via a sonic wave-assisted reaction with cysteine as an S and N dopant under acidic conditions at room temperature. As-prepared NSGO retained the same lateral size as pristine GO, but possessed a large proportion of thiopyran sulfur and pyrrolic nitrogen. In addition, NSGO exhibited highly enhanced fluorescence with strong resistance to the pH and polarity of solution. Mechanistic investigation suggested that S-doping was essential for the improved fluorescence properties of NSGO. Finally, to investigate the effect of heteroatoms-doping on sensing performance, fluorescent NSGO was employed as a fluorescent biosensor for the detection of matrix metalloproteinases 2 (MMP-2) via a peptide-induced assembly and self-quenching principle. As compared to pristine GO, NSGO assemblies exhibited more significant fluorescence quenching, but upon addition of MMP-2, the NSGO assemblies were disassembled, leading to the higher restoration of the quenched fluorescence, eventually resulting in the more sensitive detection of MMP-2.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Graphene oxide
Heteroatom doping
MMP-2
Optical biosensor
Sonic wave-assisted reaction

xocs:meta.xocs:articleinfo: articleinfo articletitlenorm authfirstinitialnorm authfirstsurnamenorm cid cids contenttype copyright crossmark dateloaded dateloadedtxt datesearch datesort dateupdated dco docsubtype doctype doi eid ewtransactionid hubeid indexeddate issn issnnorm itemstage itemtransactionid itemweight openaccess openarchive pg pgfirst pglast pii piinorm pubdatestart pubdatetxt pubyr sectiontitle sortorder srctitle srctitlenorm srctype ssids alllist content subj subheadings suppl tomb volfirst volissue volumelist webpdf webpdfpagecount yearnav figure table e-component body acknowledge affil appendices articletitle auth authfirstini authfull authkeywords authlast grantnumber grantsponsor grantsponsorid highlightsabst orcid primabst ref

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic illustration for the synthesis of sulfur-doped GO (NSGO).

Physical structure of NSGO. a) HR-TEM image and b)-d) EDS atomic mapping of pristine GO. e) HR-TEM image and f)-h) EDS atomic mapping of NSGO. AFM images of i) pristine GO and j) NSGO with height profiles. k) Size distribution of pristine GO (black dots) and NSGO (red dots). (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article).

Chemical structure of NSGO. XPS spectra of a) C1s, b) N1s, and c) S2p for NSGO. d) Relative amounts of the functional groups in pristine GO, DMF-GO, and NSGO. e) FT-IR spectra of GO (black line) and NSGO (red line). f) Proposed structure of NSGO. (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article).

Optical properties of NSGO. a) UV/Vis absorption spectra of pristine GO (black line) and NSGO (red line). b) Fluorescence spectra of pristine GO (black line), NSGO (red line), Cys-coupled GO (green line), and DMF-GO (blue line) at the concentration of 0.1 mg mL−1. c) Normalized fluorescence intensities of NSGO and the GOs treated with various amino acids. I0 and I are the fluorescence intensities of pristine GO and the GO treated with each amino acid, respectively. d) Changes in the fluorescence intensity and wavelength of pristine GO (square), NSGO (circle), and DMF-GO (triangle) over a solvent polarity. e) Changes in the fluorescence intensity of pristine GO (black), NSGO (red), and DMF-GO (blue) at 560 nm over various pH values (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article).
Schematic illustration for the detection of MMP-2 based on a NSGO assembly-disassembly sensing principle.

Turn-on fluorescence detection of MMP-2 over NSGO-peptide assemblies. a) Fluorescence spectra of NSGO-peptide hybrids before and after the formation of assemblies via disulfide formation. Fluorescence responses of b) NSGO-P1 assembly, and c) NSGO-P2 and NSGO-P3 assemblies to MMP-2 at various concentrations. d) Fluorescence recovery of NSGO-P1 assembly (red line) and pristine GO-P1 assembly (black line) in the presence of MMP-2 at various concentrations; I0 and I are the fluorescence intensities at 560 nm before and after addition of MMP-2, respectively. All spectra were obtained under excitation at 400 nm. (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article).

Peptide sequences coupled onto the surface of fluorescent NSGO for the selective detection of MMP-2. The sequences shown in blue color indicate the enzyme recognition sites for MMP-2. A red color indicates cysteine for disulfide formation.

head.ce:title: Atomically-tailored graphene oxide displaying enhanced fluorescence for the improved optical sensing of MMP-2

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemical Engineering, Hanyang University, Ansan 426-791, Republic of Korea

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemical and Biological Engineering, Seoul National University, Seoul 151-744, Republic of Korea

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemical and Biological Engineering

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Modulation of the optical properties of two-dimensional nanomaterials is a crucial step to improve their sensing performance for the optical detection of target molecules of interest. Here, we report a mild, simple and effective approach for heteroatoms-doping of graphene oxide (GO) without size alteration, resulting in enhanced fluorescence properties. Sulfur and nitrogen atoms were chemically doped onto the basal plane of GO (NSGO) via a sonic wave-assisted reaction with cysteine as an S and N dopant under acidic conditions at room temperature. As-prepared NSGO retained the same lateral size as pristine GO, but possessed a large proportion of thiopyran sulfur and pyrrolic nitrogen. In addition, NSGO exhibited highly enhanced fluorescence with strong resistance to the pH and polarity of solution. Mechanistic investigation suggested that S-doping was essential for the improved fluorescence properties of NSGO. Finally, to investigate the effect of heteroatoms-doping on sensing performance, fluorescent NSGO was employed as a fluorescent biosensor for the detection of matrix metalloproteinases 2 (MMP-2) via a peptide-induced assembly and self-quenching principle. As compared to pristine GO, NSGO assemblies exhibited more significant fluorescence quenching, but upon addition of MMP-2, the NSGO assemblies were disassembled, leading to the higher restoration of the quenched fluorescence, eventually resulting in the more sensitive detection of MMP-2.

ce:section.ce:para: Graphene oxide (GO), which is an exfoliated sheet of oxidized graphite [1], has attracted great attention in diverse research fields such as energy storage and conversion [2–4], biosensing [5–8], water purification [9], liquid crystal [10], and biomedicine [11–13] because of its intrinsic physicochemical properties. In particular, GO emits fluorescence in the visible and near-infrared (nIR) regions through its conjugated sp2 domains confined by oxygen-containing functional groups [14–19]. GO fluorescence has been found to be more resistant to photobleaching than that of organic fluorophores and tunable from visible to nIR wavelengths via adjustment of the size of the sp2 domains [20–24], along with beneficial characteristics such as biocompatibility, large surface area, facile modification, and good water dispensability. Hence, GO has been employed as a fluorescent nanomaterial in optical biosensing [5,7,8,25] and bioimaging [21,26,27]. In spite of these advantages, however, GO exhibits a low quantum yield (QY) for fluorescence [8,28], which needs to be improved for achieving better performances in optical sensing and imaging.

Heteroatom-doping is one of the effective ways to modulate the physicochemical properties of carbon nanomaterials. In particular, many efforts have been directed to improve the QYs of carbon materials such as graphene, graphene quantum dots, and carbon dots via N, S, or P-doping in various solution processes [29–39]. However, most of the heteroatom-doping approaches often require harsh reaction conditions and elevated reaction temperatures in which useful functional groups necessary for further functionalization to design biosensors can decompose. A few studies described mild strategies for S or N doping to carbon materials [40–43], but they have rarely been employed to enhance the weak fluorescence of GO. Therefore, it is of great interest to develop a mild and simple solution process for the effective doping of heteroatoms onto GO to enhance its fluorescence properties without size alteration.

Matrix metalloproteinases (MMPs) are endopeptidases capable of breaking down extracellular matrix (ECM), influencing cell behaviors in normal biological processes. Moreover, MMPs are considered to be important protein enzymes in cancer biology [44], because they are involved in cancer cell growth, differentiation, apoptosis, migration, invasion, and immune surveillance [45]. Among the MMPs, MMP-2 is known to be closely associated with the metastasis of malignant cells [46,47], making it be an important biomarker for cancer diagnosis in clinical applications. To date, several methods have been reported for the detection of MMP-2, such as zymography [48], enzyme-linked immunosorbent assay (ELISA) [49], and activity assays [50]. However, these methods often require multiple reaction steps and long assay time [51]. As an alternative to these methods, several approaches have been developed, including Förster resonance energy transfer (FRET)-based detection [5,8,52–56], electrochemical sensing [53,57], electrochemiluminescence detection [58], and photonic crystals-based sensing [59,60]. Among them, FRET-based biosensors have widely been employed for the rapid and sensitive detection of MMP-2. However, all of the FRET-based sensing platforms need to incorporate an effective pair of fluorescent donor and quencher, often leading to a multiple and complex synthetic process. Hence, there is still a demand on more simply-designed FRET biosensors for the selective detection of MMP-2.

Here, we report a simple, mild, and effective approach for the preparation of heteroatoms-doped GO without size alteration in solution, displaying enhanced fluorescence properties. The effect of S- and N-doping on the fluorescence properties of GO is thoroughly explored. Moreover, the heteroatoms-doped GO is exploited as a FRET-based fluorescent biosensor for the sensitive detection of MMP-2, and its sensing performance is compared with that of pristine GO.

A 1 mL portion of N,N-dimethylmethanamide (DMF) containing GO (1 mg mL−1) was mixed with 1 mL of l-cysteine (120 mM) dissolved in 1% HCl/water. The resulting mixture was sonicated using a probe sonicator with a pulse of 0.5 s-on/0.5 s-off at a power of 10.5 W. After mild sonication for 1 h, N,S-codoped GO (NSGO) was centrifuged (17,000 rpm for 30 min) and washed multiple times with water. NSGO was then dispersed in water at a concentration of 1 mg mL−1 for further experiments.

NSGO was conjugated with maleimide-modified peptides (Mal-Pep) through a fast reaction between the thiol groups of NSGO and maleimide groups. A 1 mL portion of NSGO aqueous solution (1 mg mL−1) was reacted with 1 mL of Mal-Pep (1.1 equiv.) in phosphate buffered saline (PBS) (20 mM, pH 6.8) for 12 h at 4 °C under vigorous shaking. The resulting NSGO-Peptide conjugate was washed with water several times via centrifugation. The NSGO-Peptide conjugate was stirred at 1400 rpm in a mixture of dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO) and water (1:1) for 12 h, for the formation of NSGO-Peptide assemblies. The resulting NSGO-Peptide assemblies were then washed with water several times before use.

MMP-2 was added to the NSGO-Peptide assembly solution (0.1 mg mL−1) in Tris-HCl buffer (50 mM, pH 7.6), and the reaction mixture was incubated for 2 h at 37 °C. The final concentration of MMP-2 varied from 0 to 10 μg mL−1. The fluorescence response of the NSGO-Peptide assembly to MMP-2 at each concentration was measured under excitation at 400 nm.

In order to obtain NSGO effectively without an alteration in size, a pulsed sonic wave-assisted doping approach using cysteine as a sulfur dopant was employed under acidic condition (Fig. 1 ). As depicted, the HCl aqueous solution containing cysteine (120 mM) was mixed with the GO/DMF solution, and the resulting mixture was then very gently sonicated (10.5 W) for 1 h to maintain the size of GO without the formation of smaller carbon dots.

As-prepared NSGO was then analyzed by transmission electron microscopy (TEM). As shown in Fig. 2 a–d, pristine GO exhibited a smooth surface not bearing sulfur and nitrogen atoms in the atomic mapping images obtained by electron energy loss spectroscopy (EELS). However, NSGO displayed sulfur and nitrogen atoms homogeneously across its whole area although it retained the smooth morphology similar to pristine GO (Fig. 2e–h and Fig. S1). These results indicate that sulfur and nitrogen atoms were successfully doped into the GO. The height and size of NSGO were then analyzed by atomic force microscopy (AFM). The average height of NSGO increased to 3.4 nm, compared with that of pristine GO (1.5 nm) as shown in Fig. 2i and j. We speculate that this height increase in NSGO might be induced by structural distortion caused by doping of the bigger sulfur atoms and conjugation of cysteine molecules at its edge. The lateral size of NSGO was determined by calculating the average values of its largest and smallest transverse widths. As shown in Fig. 2k, the size distribution of NSGO appeared to be very similar with that of pristine GO, indicating that the N and S-doing of GO occurred without a reduction of its size or the formation of smaller carbon dots.

With an increase in the sonication time from 1 h to 4 and 12 h, however, the surface of NSGO was covered with undesired small carbonaceous dots (Fig. S2). A long period of ultrasonic wave radiation can provide enough energy to cut GO into smaller carbon nanodots [61], followed by their adsorption on the GO surface. This result suggests that the mild sonic wave-assisted process was essential for the synthesis of NSGO without changes in its physical structure.

The chemical composition of NSGO was analyzed by an elemental analyzer. As shown in Table S1, the S and N contents of NSGO significantly increased to 23.3 and 10.4 wt%, respectively, compared with the traces of pristine GO. The details of chemical bonds in NSGO were further identified using X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS). NSGO exhibited graphitic CC (284.4 eV), C−S (286.1 eV), CN (287.4 eV), and CO (289.2 eV) bonds in its deconvoluted C1s spectrum (Fig. 3 a). NSGO possessed two N species, including pyridinic N (398.8 eV) and pyrrolic N (400.5 eV) (Fig. 3b). In addition, the SH (S2p3/2 162.9 and S2p1/2 164.0 eV) and CSC (S2p3/2 164.1 and S2p1/2 165.2 eV) bonds were clearly observed in the S2p XPS spectrum of NSGO (Fig. 3c) [39].

The Raman spectrum of NSGO also supports doping of S and N atoms to GO under mild sonic wave radiation (Fig. S3). The relative intensity of the D and G bands of NSGO (ID/IG) at 1345 and 1581 cm−1 increased to 1.01 as compared to that of pristine GO (0.99), indicating the formation of more defects and heteroatom-doping [62,63]. These XPS and Raman results reveal that sulfur as well as nitrogen atoms were successfully doped into pristine GO, resulting in the formation of NSGO without an alteration in its size.

For comparison, GO was sonicated only in the DMF/HCl solution not containing cysteine (denoted “DMF-GO”). Next, DMF-GO and pristine GO were also analyzed by XPS (Fig. S4), and the content of their functional groups was compared with that of NSGO. As shown in Fig. 3d, the CS bond content increased significantly in NSGO compared with pristine GO and DMF-GO. Note that the amount of a CO bond in NSGO was dramatically reduced compared with pristine GO. This noticeable decrease indicates that cysteine would react with the epoxide and carboxylic acid groups of pristine GO under acidic conditions during a sonication process. It is interesting that the contents of CN and CN bonds, along with graphitic C, increased sufficiently in DMF-GO. It was reported that DMF can be hydrolyzed into dimethylamine and formic acid under acidic conditions [64]. Therefore, it can be expected that this resulting hydrolyzed product, dimethylamine, could react with the functional groups of GO, leading to N-doping of pristine GO even in the absence of cysteine.

For N species, the newly formed N bonds were clearly observed in both NSGO and DMF-GO, whereas there were no noticeable N atoms in pristine GO. Note that a majority of N species in NSGO was pyrrolic, while DMF-GO included both pyrrolic and pyridinic N atoms in similar proportion. The lone pair electrons of the pyrrolic N atoms can be involved more effectively in the conjugation system of the π electrons of graphitic sp2 C, resulting in a larger increase in the electron density of NSGO than DMF-GO. For S species, NSGO possessed a large portion of sp2 CSC bonds in addition to SH bonds. This CSC species indicates that sulfur atoms were doped in the basal plane of pristine GO during the reaction, which can lead to a significant increase in the electron density of NSGO. The changes in the electron density could influence the fluorescence properties of NSGO as investigated. Such an S-doping effect, however, is not likely to be observed in DMF-GO or pristine GO.

The chemical structure of NSGO was also identified using Fourier-transform infrared (FT-IR) spectroscopy. As shown in Fig. 3e, the vibrational modes corresponding to pyrrolic CN (959.4 cm−1), pyridinic CN (1619.8 cm−1), CC (1587.3 cm−1), aromatic CN (1194.6 cm−1), amide CN (1405.9 cm−1), and SH (2588.1 cm−1) bonds were distinctly observed in the FT-IR spectrum of NSGO while pristine GO did not display them. In addition, the intensity ratio between the newly formed SH bond and the conjugated CC bond on NSGO increased as a function of reaction time (Fig. S5), indicating that the N,S-doping and the formation of the new chemical bonds proceeded during the reaction. On the basis of the XPS and FT-IR analytical results, we propose that the chemical structure of NSGO (Fig. 3f). NSGO includes thiopyran, thiol, pyrrolic and pyridinic nitrogen, graphitic carbon, and amide groups. Note that the carboxyl groups almost disappeared in NSGO. The changes in the chemical structure of NSGO can dramatically induce the enhancement of its fluorescence.

The optical properties of NSGO were investigated. Fig. 4 a and Fig. S6 shows the UV/Vis absorption spectra of pristine GO and NSGO dispersed in an aqueous solution. It was found that the two peaks of NSGO corresponding to π-π* and n-π* electronic transitions appeared at shorter wavelengths, 195 and 245 nm, respectively, compared with pristine GO (230 and 303 nm). This blue shift can be attributed to the fact that the N atoms doped in NSGO have a stronger electronic affinity than carbon [43,65], requiring higher energies to make the electrons excited. In addition, NSGO exhibited more intense absorption than pristine GO in the visible range from 400 to 800 nm, indicating that the electron density of the sp2 conjugation domains in NSGO significantly increased.

The fluorescence of NSGO was then measured under excitation at 400 nm and compared with pristine GO and DMF-GO. As shown in Fig. 4b, the fluorescence intensity of NSGO (red line) was one order of magnitude higher than that of pristine GO (black line). Besides, NSGO exhibited a fluorescence five times as intense as DMF-GO (blue line), indicating that S-doping is essential for the large enhancement of GO fluorescence. Note that when cysteine was coupled to the carboxylic acid of pristine GO using the coupling reagents such as 1-ethyl-3-(3-dimethylaminopropyl)carbodiimide and N-hydroxysuccinimide, its fluorescence rarely increased (Fig. 4b, green line). This result suggests that the effective S-doping on the basal plane of pristine GO was a crucial factor for the fluorescence enhancement rather than the coupling of cysteine to the carboxylic acid of GO. The significant enhancement in the fluorescence of NSGO can be induced by two factors. First, a large amount of S atoms as well as pyrrolic N atoms were effectively doped on its basal plane, as shown in Fig. 3. N, S-codoping would make NSGO have a different surface state in which many trapped electrons could have a larger probability for radiative recombination, resulting in its more enhanced fluorescence than DMF-GO and pristine GO [31,66,67]. Second, NSGO had no noticeable carboxyl groups, which have been known to be a surface state for non-radiative recombination of localized electron-hole pairs [16,18], resulting in a further increase in the probability of radiative recombination for fluorescence emission in NSGO.

Next, the effects of the sonication time, the concentration of cysteine, and the type of amino acid on the fluorescence enhancement were investigated. When the sonication time for the synthesis of NSGO in the HCl/DMF solution containing 40 mM of cysteine was increased from 1 h to 4 and 12 h, its fluorescence increased (Fig. S7b), but it significantly diminished beyond 120 mM of cysteine. This fluorescence decrease could be attributed to much more formation of defects at higher concentration of cysteine, which was supported by an increase in the ID/IG ratio of NSGO synthesized at 200 mM of cysteine (Fig. S3, blue line). Based on these experimental results, we found the optimal reaction conditions (1 h and 120 mM of cysteine), in which the fluorescence of NSGO was significantly enhanced without a size alteration and the formation of small carbon dots. Instead of cysteine, nineteen different natural amino acids, including arginine, tyrosine, and methionine, were added into the HCl/DMF solution during sonication, and the fluorescence of the resulting GOs was measured. As shown in Fig. 4c, the other amino acids except cysteine were not effective to improve the poor fluorescence of pristine GO. The fluorescence of GO was hardly enhanced. This result reveals that the effective S-doping of GO using cysteine as a primary S source in the sonic wave-assisted process is imperative for the fluorescence enhancement.

Subsequently, solvatochromic effects on the NSGO fluorescence were investigated. As shown in Fig. 4d, the fluorescence of NSGO was not much influenced by solvent polarities, whereas pristine GO and DMF-GO exhibited clear solvatochromic effects on their fluorescence. The fluorescence intensity and emission wavelength of NSGO remained almost constant over all the solvent polarities (circle), indicating that NSGO displays similar solvation aspects regardless of the types of the solvents. However, the fluorescence of pristine GO (square) and DMF-GO (triangle) was red-shifted as the solvent polarity increased, indicating that the excited states of GO or DMF-GO underwent stronger interactions with the dipoles of the polar solvents, followed by relaxation for fluorescence emission [66,68]. Next, the pH dependency of NSGO fluorescence was explored. As shown in Fig. 4e, the fluorescence intensity of NSGO remained constant in the pH range of 2−9 and showed a slight decrease in the pH range of 10−11. However, the fluorescence of pristine GO and DMF-GO was diminished significantly with increasing pH value of the solution. It was previously reported that the pH-dependent quenching of GO fluorescence was caused by deprotonation of the carboxyl groups of GO at excited states [19,66]. By contrast, NSGO contains no carboxyl groups on its basal plane and edge as discussed in the chemical structure of NSGO (Fig. 3), which makes its fluorescence pH-independent. These results show that heteroatoms-doping imparts unique and enhanced fluorescence properties to NSGO. In addition, all the experimental results clearly suggest that the optical properties of fluorescent nanomaterials including GO rely on their chemical structures, which can be modulated by heteroatoms-doping, a dopant type, and a dopant content.

We finally investigated the effect of heteroatoms-doping on the fluorescent detection of MMP-2 by comparing the sensing performance of NSGO with that of pristine GO. To this end, the peptide-induced assembly and self-quenching principle, which we previously developed [5], were employed for MMP-2 detection. As shown in Fig. 5 , the NSGO assemblies formed via disulfide bonds between the cysteine-containing peptides anchored on NSGO, resulting in its fluorescence-quenching. Then, in the presence of MMP-2, the NSGO assemblies were disassembled into individual NSGOs, inducing the recovery of the self-quenched fluorescence of the NSGO. Based on this turn-on fluorescence of the NSGO, MMP-2 can be detected.

Three different peptides bearing a maleimide group at their N terminal for coupling them to the thiol groups of NSGO were prepared (Table 1 ). The first peptide (denoted “P1″) consists of a hydrophilic and stretchable fragment (KKPPPPK), a MMP-2 recognition sequence (GPLGVRG), and a cysteine group at its C terminal for the formation of disulfide bonds. The second peptide (denoted “P2”) contains the same sequence as P1, except a cysteine group at the C terminal. Since P2 does not have cysteine at its C terminal, it should not induce the assembly of NSGO via disulfide bond formation. The third one (denoted “P3”) does not include a MMP-2 recognition fragment, but has a cysteine group at its C terminal. Therefore, P3 can enable NSGO to be assembled, but the resulting NSGO-P3 assemblies cannot recognize MMP-2. Each peptide was then introduced onto NSGO through the fast reaction between the thiol groups of NSGO and the maleimide moiety of the peptide (Fig. S8). The NSGO conjugated with P1 (NSGO-P1) was effectively obtained as a fluorescent biosensor for the selective detection of MMP-2. In addition, two more NSGO/peptide conjugates (NSGO-P2 and NSGO-P3) were readily synthesized for control experiments. The surface modification of NSGO was simpler, faster, and more effective than that of pristine GO because NSGO has free thiol groups for the reaction with maleimide. In the previous study [5], GO required multi-step procedures for immobilization of peptides, but NSGO needed only the single reaction to obtain NSGO/peptide conjugates.

NSGO/peptide conjugates exhibited very strong fluorescence at the early stage of dispersion (Fig. 6 a). However, while NSGO-P1 or NSGO-P3 was dispersed in DMSO for 2 h at RT, both conjugates started being spontaneously assembled via disulfide bonds, resulting in the dramatic quenching of the NSGO fluorescence (Fig. 6a, NSGO-P1 assembly and NSGO-P3 assembly). As expected, NSGO-P2, not bearing cysteine, was not assembled, and its fluorescence remained almost the same throughout the dispersion for 2 h. These results clearly show that NSGO assemblies were induced by the formation of disulfide bonds between the cysteine groups anchored on the NSGO and the assembly of the NSGO caused significant fluorescence quenching.

Next, the sensing performance of NSGO-P1 assembly for MMP-2 detection was examined. As shown in Fig. 6b, the quenched fluorescence of the NSGO-P1 assembly was gradually restored when the concentration of MMP-2 in the solution increased. This result suggests that the NSGO-P1 assembly was able to detect MMP-2 based on its restored fluorescence after cleavage of the peptide by the MMP-2 enzyme. On the other hand, the fluorescence of NSGO-P3 assembly not bearing the MMP-2-recognizing fragment was not restored in the presence of MMP-2 at the highest concentration (10 μg mL−1) (Fig. 6c). Moreover, NSGO-P2 showed no fluorescence change upon addition of MMP-2. These control experimental results again reveal that the fluorescence restoration of the NSGO-P1 assembly was caused via the selective recognition of MMP-2, followed by its disassembly.

In order to emphasize the benefit of using more fluorescent NSGO in MMP-2 detection, P1 was also coupled to pristine GO (denoted “GO-P1”), and the resulting GO-P1 assembly was employed as a sensing platform for MMP-2 detection for comparison. As shown in Fig. 6d, the fluorescence on-off response of the NSGO-P1 assembly in the presence of MMP-2 was greater than that of the GO-P1 assembly. Note that the NSGO-P1 assembly showed a much more distinct increase in its fluorescence at low concentrations of MMP-2 (< 0.1 μg mL−1) than the GO-P1. This result indicates that the NSGO-P1 assembly was able to detect MMP-2 more sensitively than the GO-P1 assembly. The NSGO-P1 assembly detected MMP-2 at concentrations as low as 10 ng mL−1. This improved sensing performance of the NSGO assembly biosensor in MMP-2 detection can arise from the enhanced optical properties of NSGO modulated by heteroatoms-doping. When more fluorescent NSGO was assembled, its fluorescence quenching was found to be more significant (61%) than that of pristine GO (42%) through homo-FRET mechanism [69–71] (Fig. S9). This effective fluorescence quenching in the NSGO-P1 assembly can be caused by the enhanced absorption of NSGO in a visible range, as compared with pristine GO (Fig. 4a and S4). The other factor for the improved sensing performance of the NSGO assembly biosensor is the higher resistance of NSGO fluorescence to dielectric environment changes (Fig. 4d and e). After the NSGO assembly was dis-assembled by MMP-2, the fluorescence of the dispersed NSGO would be less susceptible to perturbation in the dielectric environment, resulting in the more effective fluorescence turn-on. The limit of detection (LOD) of NSGO-P1 assembly and GO-P1 assembly was calculated based on the standard deviation of the response (SD) and the slope of the calibration curve (S) with the according formula: LOD = 3.3(SD/S) [72]. As a result, the NSGO-P1 assembly was able to detect MMP-2 ten times more sensitively (40 ng mL−1) along with a wider dynamic range than the pristine GO-P1 assembly. As compared to the sensing performance of FRET-based nanosensors previously reported (Table S2), the NSGO assembly sensor displayed a compatible LOD with a broader dynamic range for MMP-2 detection. In particular, it is clear that the improved fluorescence of NSGO allowed the more sensitive detection of MMP-2 at a broader range of concentrations as compared to the pristine GO-based nanosensor.

A mild, simple, and effective approach based on the sonic wave-assisted reaction with cysteine was developed for the synthesis of N,S-codoped GO with enhanced fluorescence properties. As-prepared NSGO retained the same lateral size as pristine GO, but was doped with a large proportion of thiopyran sulfur and pyrrolic nitrogen atoms. NSGO exhibited highly enhanced fluorescence with strong resistance to the pH and polarity of solution. Fluorescent NSGO was successfully exploited as a FRET-based biosensor for the sensitive detection of MMP-2 based on a peptide-mediated NSGO assembly-disassembly sensing principle. In addition, NSGO showed a much better sensing performance in MMP-2 detection than pristine GO. The heteroatoms-doping strategy can be extended to designing a variety of nanomaterials doped with sulfur and nitrogen under mild conditions. In addition, a NSGO-based optical sensing principle can be utilized for the selective and sensitive detection of many different proteases.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This study was supported by the research fund of Hanyang University (HY-2017-P).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary material related to this article can be found, in the online version, at doi:https://doi.org/10.1016/j.snb.2018.12.100.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following is Supplementary data to this article:

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Coupled molybdenum carbide and reduced graphene oxide electrocatalysts for efficient hydrogen evolution
Nickel cobalt hydroxide@ reduced graphene oxide hybrid nanolayers for high performance asymmetric supercapacitors with remarkable cycling stability
Hierarchical nanocomposites of polyaniline nanowire arrays on graphene oxide sheets with synergistic effect for energy storage
Proteolytic disassembly of peptide-mediated graphene oxide assemblies for turn-on fluorescence sensing of proteases
An electrochemical nanobiosensor for plasma miRNA-155, based on graphene oxide and gold nanorod, for early detection of breast cancer
Chemically-modulated photoluminescence of graphene oxide for selective detection of neurotransmitter by “turn-on” response
Luminescent graphene oxide with a peptide‐quencher complex for optical detection of cell‐secreted proteases by a turn‐on response
Graphene oxide (GO) enhanced polyamide (PA) thin-film nanocomposite (TFN) membrane for water purification
Graphene oxide liquid crystals: discovery, evolution and applications
Optical detection of enzymatic activity and inhibitors on non-covalently functionalized fluorescent graphene oxide
Ultrasmall reduced graphene oxide with high near-infrared absorbance for photothermal therapy
Noncovalent functionalization of graphene and graphene oxide for energy materials, biosensing, catalytic, and biomedical applications
Making graphene luminescent by oxygen plasma treatment
Graphene oxide as a chemically tunable platform for optical applications
Photoluminescence and band gap modulation in graphene oxide
